questions.scenario: A logistics company is building an AI assistant that needs to check inventory levels, update shipping status, and calculate delivery estimates. The assistant must autonomously decide which operations to perform based on user requests. All data sources are internal APIs with existing REST endpoints.
questions.question: Which approach provides the MOST flexible architecture for enabling the AI assistant to autonomously execute multiple operations?

options.text: Create a single Lambda function that handles all operations and invoke it directly from Amazon Bedrock
Configure Amazon Bedrock Agents with Action Groups, defining each operation as a separate action with OpenAPI schemas
Build a custom orchestration layer using Step Functions to route user requests to appropriate endpoints
Pre-compute all possible operation combinations and store them in a lookup table

questions.explanation: Amazon Bedrock Agents with Action Groups is designed for exactly this use case. Each operation (inventory check, shipping update, delivery calculation) becomes an action defined by an OpenAPI schema. The agent's ReAct-style orchestration autonomously decides which actions to invoke based on user intent.

incorrectExplanations.a: A single Lambda function doesn't provide the autonomous decision-making capability. The FM wouldn't know when or how to invoke it.
incorrectExplanations.c: Step Functions provides orchestration but requires predefined workflows. It doesn't provide the autonomous, intent-based operation selection that agents offer.
incorrectExplanations.d: Pre-computing combinations doesn't scale and can't handle novel user requests that weren't anticipated.

eliminationHints: Step Functions = predefined workflows, not autonomous
Pre-compute = doesn't scale, can't handle novel requests
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Autonomous multi-operation execution = Bedrock Agents with Action Groups

questions.examTip: Questions about autonomous tool execution or multi-step operations point to Bedrock Agents with Action Groups.

questions.scenario: A research organization is developing an AI assistant that needs to search academic databases, query internal knowledge bases, and access real-time experiment data. The assistant will run on researchers' local machines and must be able to connect to different data sources depending on the researcher's project. The organization wants to add new data sources without modifying the AI application code.
questions.question: Which architecture enables researchers to connect to project-specific data sources without application code changes?

options.text: Deploy Amazon Bedrock Agents with pre-configured Action Groups for each possible data source
Implement Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers for each data source, allowing dynamic server discovery and connection
Create a unified API gateway that routes requests to different backends based on project configuration
Use Lambda functions with environment variables to switch between data sources

questions.explanation: Model Context Protocol (MCP) provides a standardized interface for connecting AI assistants to external tools and data sources. MCP servers can be discovered and connected dynamically without modifying the AI application. Each data source runs as an MCP server, and researchers can connect to project-specific servers as needed.

incorrectExplanations.a: Pre-configured Action Groups require defining all sources upfront and don't support dynamic discovery. Adding new sources requires agent reconfiguration.
incorrectExplanations.c: A unified API gateway centralizes control but requires gateway modifications for new data sources. It doesn't provide the local, dynamic connection capability described.
incorrectExplanations.d: Lambda environment variables require redeployment to change. This doesn't meet the 'without code changes' requirement.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Dynamic data source connection without code changes = MCP

questions.examTip: MCP enables standardized, dynamic tool connections. Think MCP when you see 'dynamic data source connection' or 'local AI assistant with external tools.'

questions.scenario: A customer support platform uses Amazon Bedrock Agents to handle customer inquiries. The agent needs to access customer account information, process refunds, and escalate to human agents when necessary. The operations have different security requirements: viewing account info requires basic authentication, processing refunds requires elevated privileges, and escalation requires manager approval.
questions.question: How should the team configure the agent to enforce different authorization levels for each operation?

options.text: Create separate Bedrock Agents for each security level, routing requests to the appropriate agent
Configure Action Groups with Lambda functions that verify authorization using the user context passed through session attributes
Use a single Action Group with all operations and handle authorization in the FM prompt
Store authorization levels in a DynamoDB table and have the agent query it before each operation

questions.explanation: Lambda functions backing Action Groups can access session attributes containing user context. Each Lambda function verifies the user's authorization level before executing its operation. This provides proper authorization enforcement at the action level without relying on the FM to make security decisions.

incorrectExplanations.a: Multiple agents adds complexity and doesn't solve the authorization problem—you'd still need to verify permissions within each agent.
incorrectExplanations.c: Never rely on FM prompts for security decisions. Prompts can be manipulated, and FMs don't enforce security—they generate text.
incorrectExplanations.d: Querying authorization separately adds latency and still requires enforcement logic. The Lambda approach combines lookup and enforcement.

eliminationHints: Multiple agents = doesn't solve auth problem
FM prompt = never use for security decisions
Separate query = inefficient, still needs enforcement
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Authorization enforcement = Lambda functions with user context from session attributes

questions.examTip: Security decisions must be made in code (Lambda), not in FM prompts. Session attributes carry user context to Lambda functions.

questions.scenario: A media company needs to deploy a text generation model for real-time content creation. The model will receive variable traffic throughout the day, with peaks during morning and evening hours. Cost efficiency is important, but response latency must remain under 2 seconds even during peak times.
questions.question: Which deployment configuration balances cost efficiency with consistent latency during variable traffic?

options.text: Use Amazon Bedrock on-demand inference for all traffic to minimize costs during low-traffic periods
Deploy on Amazon SageMaker with a fixed number of instances sized for peak traffic
Use Amazon Bedrock Provisioned Throughput for baseline traffic with on-demand for overflow during peaks
Deploy on Amazon EC2 Spot Instances to minimize costs with Auto Scaling for peak handling

questions.explanation: Provisioned Throughput provides guaranteed capacity with consistent latency for baseline traffic. On-demand handles overflow during peaks without over-provisioning. This hybrid approach optimizes cost while maintaining latency SLAs.

incorrectExplanations.a: On-demand alone may have variable latency during high-traffic periods as resources are shared. It doesn't guarantee the 2-second latency requirement.
incorrectExplanations.b: Fixed SageMaker instances sized for peak means paying for unused capacity during low-traffic periods, reducing cost efficiency.
incorrectExplanations.d: Spot Instances can be interrupted, which is unacceptable for real-time content creation. This introduces reliability issues.

eliminationHints: Fixed peak sizing = wasted capacity during low traffic
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Consistent latency with cost efficiency = Provisioned Throughput baseline + on-demand overflow

questions.examTip: Provisioned Throughput guarantees capacity and latency. Combine with on-demand for cost-effective handling of traffic spikes.

questions.scenario: A multinational company is deploying a GenAI application that must serve users in North America, Europe, and Asia-Pacific. The application uses Amazon Bedrock for text generation. Users report inconsistent latency, with some regions experiencing significantly slower responses than others. The team wants to minimize latency for all users without deploying separate infrastructure in each region.
questions.question: Which Bedrock feature addresses multi-region latency without requiring separate regional deployments?

options.text: Enable Amazon CloudFront caching for Bedrock API responses to serve from edge locations
Configure Cross-Region Inference to automatically route requests to available regions with capacity
Deploy the application in a single central region and use AWS Global Accelerator for network optimization
Create VPC peering connections between regions to reduce network latency

questions.explanation: Cross-Region Inference in Amazon Bedrock automatically routes requests to available regions, reducing latency by selecting closer or less-loaded regions. This provides multi-region optimization without deploying separate infrastructure in each region.

incorrectExplanations.a: CloudFront caching doesn't work well for dynamic GenAI responses, which vary per request. Caching is effective for static or predictable content.
incorrectExplanations.c: Global Accelerator improves network path but doesn't reduce inference latency. The inference still happens in the single central region.
incorrectExplanations.d: VPC peering connects VPCs but doesn't address FM inference latency. It's for private network connectivity, not performance optimization.

parseStrategy.keyPhrase: multi-region latency without separate regional deployments
eliminationHints: CloudFront = for static content, not dynamic GenAI
Global Accelerator = network path only, not inference
VPC peering = private connectivity, not latency optimization
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Multi-region FM latency optimization = Cross-Region Inference

questions.examTip: Cross-Region Inference is Bedrock's answer to global latency challenges. It routes to available regions automatically.

questions.scenario: An enterprise is building an internal GenAI chatbot that needs to access company databases containing sensitive employee information. The security team requires that all communication between the application and Amazon Bedrock remain within the AWS network without traversing the public internet.
questions.question: Which configuration ensures private connectivity between the application and Amazon Bedrock?

options.text: Configure AWS Direct Connect from the corporate data center to the VPC hosting the application
Create an Interface VPC Endpoint for Amazon Bedrock in the application's VPC
Deploy the application in a private subnet with a NAT Gateway for outbound Bedrock access
Use AWS PrivateLink between the corporate network and a Bedrock-dedicated VPC

questions.explanation: Interface VPC Endpoints (powered by AWS PrivateLink) create private connectivity to AWS services without using public IPs or traversing the internet. Creating a Bedrock endpoint in the VPC allows private communication from the application to Bedrock.

incorrectExplanations.a: Direct Connect provides private connectivity from on-premises to AWS but doesn't address the VPC-to-Bedrock communication, which would still go over the internet without a VPC endpoint.
incorrectExplanations.c: NAT Gateway enables internet access from private subnets. Traffic through NAT Gateway still traverses the public internet, violating the requirement.
incorrectExplanations.d: PrivateLink is the underlying technology, but you implement it through Interface VPC Endpoints. This option describes the mechanism, not the correct configuration approach.

eliminationHints: Direct Connect = on-premises connectivity, not VPC-to-service
NAT Gateway = still uses public internet
Generic PrivateLink = VPC Endpoint is the implementation
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Private VPC-to-AWS-service connectivity = Interface VPC Endpoint

questions.examTip: VPC Endpoints provide private connectivity to AWS services. Interface Endpoints (PrivateLink) for most services including Bedrock.

questions.scenario: A financial services firm is integrating Amazon Bedrock into their existing risk analysis platform. The platform uses Amazon API Gateway for external APIs and internal microservices communicate through Amazon EventBridge. The GenAI capability should be accessible through the same API Gateway as other services, with consistent authentication, rate limiting, and monitoring.
questions.question: Which integration pattern provides the MOST consistent user experience while leveraging existing platform capabilities?

options.text: Create a direct integration from API Gateway to Amazon Bedrock using AWS service integrations
Deploy a Lambda function behind API Gateway that proxies requests to Bedrock, applying platform-standard middleware
Configure EventBridge to publish Bedrock requests and subscribe Lambda functions for processing
Use API Gateway WebSocket APIs for direct client-to-Bedrock streaming connections

questions.explanation: Lambda behind API Gateway allows you to apply the same authentication, rate limiting, and monitoring patterns used for other platform services. The Lambda function can add platform-specific headers, logging, and transformations while proxying to Bedrock, maintaining consistency with existing services.

incorrectExplanations.a: Direct service integration bypasses the ability to apply consistent middleware logic. You lose the platform-standard authentication and monitoring patterns.
incorrectExplanations.c: EventBridge is for event-driven async patterns, not synchronous API responses. This changes the interaction model from the existing platform approach.
incorrectExplanations.d: WebSocket APIs change the client integration pattern. The requirement is consistency with existing REST API patterns.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Platform consistency = Lambda middleware applying existing patterns

questions.examTip: Lambda provides flexibility to apply custom middleware logic. Use it when you need to wrap Bedrock calls with platform-specific patterns.

questions.scenario: A news organization is building a real-time headline summarization service. Articles arrive continuously, and summarized headlines must be generated within 100ms of article receipt. The service must handle 1000 articles per minute during peak news cycles.
questions.question: Which invocation pattern provides the LOWEST latency for high-throughput summarization?

options.text: Use Amazon Bedrock synchronous InvokeModel API with Lambda concurrency set to 1000
Implement Amazon Bedrock streaming with InvokeModelWithResponseStream, processing first tokens immediately
Batch articles in groups of 10 using Step Functions and process batches sequentially
Queue articles in SQS and use a single Lambda function to process them in order

questions.explanation: For very short outputs like headlines, synchronous InvokeModel provides lowest total latency. Streaming adds overhead for establishing the stream, which isn't beneficial for short responses. High Lambda concurrency handles the throughput requirement with parallel processing.

incorrectExplanations.b: Streaming optimizes perceived latency for long responses by delivering first tokens quickly. For short headlines, the stream setup overhead makes it slower than synchronous calls.
incorrectExplanations.c: Sequential batch processing increases latency significantly. Each article waits for the previous batch to complete, violating the 100ms requirement.
incorrectExplanations.d: Single Lambda processing creates a bottleneck. Processing in order means articles queue up, increasing latency during high-volume periods.

eliminationHints: Streaming = overhead not worth it for short outputs
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Short outputs + low latency = synchronous with high concurrency. Long outputs + perceived latency = streaming.

questions.examTip: Streaming improves perceived latency for long responses. For short outputs, synchronous calls are often faster overall.

questions.scenario: A legal tech company processes large contract documents (50+ pages) and needs to extract key terms, identify risks, and generate summaries. Document processing is not time-sensitive—users upload documents and expect results within a few hours. Cost optimization is the primary concern.
questions.question: Which invocation pattern provides the MOST cost-effective processing for large documents?

options.text: Use Amazon Bedrock batch inference, submitting documents in batches and retrieving results when processing completes
Process documents synchronously using Lambda with 15-minute timeout and maximum memory allocation
Stream document processing using InvokeModelWithResponseStream to get incremental results
Break documents into pages and process each page in parallel using Lambda concurrent invocations

questions.explanation: Batch inference provides the lowest per-token cost for non-time-sensitive workloads. Bedrock batch inference processes documents asynchronously, optimizing for throughput over latency. This matches the few-hours expectation while minimizing costs.

incorrectExplanations.b: Synchronous Lambda processing is more expensive than batch for large volumes. It also risks timeouts for very long documents.
incorrectExplanations.c: Streaming doesn't reduce cost—it reduces perceived latency. For non-time-sensitive batch processing, streaming adds unnecessary complexity.
incorrectExplanations.d: Parallel page processing increases costs (more invocations) and doesn't leverage batch pricing. It also loses document context across pages.

eliminationHints: Synchronous Lambda = higher cost, timeout risk
Parallel pages = more invocations, loses context

questions.examTip: Batch inference is the cost-optimization answer for non-time-sensitive workloads. Think batch when you see 'hours acceptable' and 'cost is primary concern.'

questions.scenario: A conversational AI application maintains context across user interactions. Each conversation can span multiple hours with intermittent messages. The application needs to include relevant conversation history with each request while managing token usage efficiently.
questions.question: Which technique BEST balances context retention with token efficiency for long conversations?

options.text: Include the full conversation history with every request to maintain complete context
Store conversation summaries, including recent messages in full and older messages as summaries
Use only the last 5 messages and discard all earlier conversation history
Store conversation in DynamoDB and retrieve the full history only when context seems relevant

questions.explanation: Summarizing older messages while keeping recent messages in full balances context retention with token efficiency. This 'rolling window with summary' approach maintains conversational continuity without linearly growing token usage as conversations lengthen.

incorrectExplanations.a: Full history grows without bound, eventually exceeding context limits and dramatically increasing costs for long conversations.
incorrectExplanations.c: Discarding history loses important context. Users may reference earlier topics, and the model won't understand.
incorrectExplanations.d: Retrieving full history when 'relevant' is hard to determine and still risks token overflow. This doesn't address the efficiency requirement.

parseStrategy.keyPhrase: balance context retention with token efficiency
eliminationHints: Full retrieval = still has overflow risk
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Long conversation context management = rolling window with summarization

questions.examTip: Context pruning with summarization is the standard pattern for long conversations. Keep recent turns detailed, older turns summarized.

questions.scenario: A company wants to deploy an AI assistant that can answer questions about internal company policies, HR procedures, and technical documentation. The content is spread across SharePoint, Confluence, and internal wikis. They want employees to interact through their existing Slack workspace.
questions.question: Which AWS service provides the MOST streamlined deployment for this enterprise knowledge assistant use case?

options.text: Build a custom RAG pipeline using Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases, Lambda, and deploy a Slack bot
Deploy Amazon Q Business with connectors for SharePoint, Confluence, and integrate with Slack
Use Amazon Kendra for search with custom Lambda functions to format responses for Slack
Index documents in OpenSearch and build custom embeddings pipeline for semantic search

questions.explanation: Amazon Q Business is purpose-built for enterprise knowledge assistants. It provides pre-built connectors for common enterprise sources (SharePoint, Confluence) and integrates with workplace tools like Slack. This is significantly faster to deploy than building custom RAG pipelines.

incorrectExplanations.a: Custom RAG pipelines work but require significantly more development effort. Q Business provides this functionality out of the box.
incorrectExplanations.c: Kendra provides search but requires custom code for conversational AI capabilities. Q Business includes the full stack.
incorrectExplanations.d: Custom OpenSearch implementation is the most effort-intensive option. It requires building everything from scratch.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Enterprise knowledge assistant with common sources = Amazon Q Business

questions.examTip: Amazon Q Business is the turnkey enterprise AI assistant. Think Q Business when you see enterprise knowledge bases + workplace integration.

questions.scenario: A software development team wants to accelerate code reviews and improve code quality. They use VS Code as their primary IDE and want AI assistance that understands their codebase, can suggest improvements, and generate unit tests based on existing patterns.
questions.question: Which AWS service provides contextual AI assistance within the development environment?

options.text: Deploy Amazon Q Business with a custom connector for the code repository
Use Amazon CodeWhisperer (Amazon Q Developer) integrated with VS Code
Build a custom VS Code extension that calls Amazon Bedrock for code suggestions
Use Amazon SageMaker to train a custom code generation model on the codebase

questions.explanation: Amazon Q Developer (formerly CodeWhisperer) provides IDE-integrated AI assistance for developers. It understands code context, suggests completions, generates tests, and integrates directly with VS Code. It's purpose-built for the developer assistance use case.

incorrectExplanations.a: Q Business is for enterprise knowledge assistants, not IDE-integrated developer tools. Wrong product for this use case.
incorrectExplanations.c: Custom extension requires significant development. Q Developer provides this functionality out of the box with optimized code understanding.
incorrectExplanations.d: Custom model training is overkill and expensive. Q Developer already has strong code understanding capabilities.

parseStrategy.keyPhrase: AI assistance within the development environment
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: IDE-integrated developer AI assistance = Amazon Q Developer

questions.examTip: Amazon Q Developer is for developers in IDEs. Amazon Q Business is for business users asking questions about company knowledge.

questions.scenario: A healthcare application uses Amazon Bedrock to analyze patient symptoms and suggest potential diagnoses. The application must integrate with an existing FHIR-compliant health records system through HL7 FHIR APIs. Patient data must never leave the organization's AWS account, and all API integrations must be auditable.
questions.question: Which architecture ensures data sovereignty while enabling FHIR API integration?

options.text: Use Bedrock in the organization's account with Lambda functions that call FHIR APIs through API Gateway with CloudTrail logging
Deploy the FHIR system in a separate AWS account and use cross-account IAM roles for access
Use Amazon HealthLake to convert FHIR data to a proprietary format that Bedrock can process directly
Replicate patient data to an S3 bucket that Bedrock can access through Knowledge Bases

questions.explanation: Lambda functions calling FHIR APIs keep patient data within the organization's processing flow. API Gateway provides the integration layer with request/response logging. CloudTrail captures API activity for audit requirements. Data stays in the organization's account throughout.

incorrectExplanations.b: Cross-account access doesn't address the data sovereignty concern—data would be accessed from another account. The requirement is data never leaves the organization's account.
incorrectExplanations.c: HealthLake doesn't convert FHIR to a 'proprietary format.' It's a FHIR-compliant service. This answer misrepresents the service and doesn't address the integration pattern.
incorrectExplanations.d: Replicating patient data to S3 creates additional copies of sensitive data, increasing data governance complexity. It's better to access through APIs.

parseStrategy.keyPhrase: data sovereignty + FHIR API integration + auditable
eliminationHints: S3 replication = data copies, governance issues
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Data sovereignty with external API integration = Lambda API calls with CloudTrail auditing

questions.examTip: Data sovereignty questions require keeping data in the organization's account. Lambda as an integration layer maintains control.

questions.scenario: An e-commerce company is building an AI shopping assistant that helps customers find products, answer questions, and complete purchases. The assistant needs to search the product catalog, check inventory, apply discounts, and process payments. Some operations (like search) are fast, while others (like payment processing) may take several seconds. Users should see immediate feedback during long-running operations.
questions.question: How should the agent handle operations with varying completion times to maintain responsive user experience?

options.text: Configure all Action Groups to return responses asynchronously using SQS, polling for completion
Use return of control in Bedrock Agents for long-running operations, streaming status updates to the user
Set short timeouts on all Lambda functions and retry failed operations automatically
Pre-compute all possible operation results during off-peak hours

questions.explanation: Return of control allows the agent to pause execution, return status to the application for long-running operations, and resume when the operation completes. The application can stream status updates to users while waiting, then continue the agent conversation with the result.

incorrectExplanations.a: Full async with polling changes the conversational model. Users would need to poll for every response, degrading the experience.
incorrectExplanations.c: Short timeouts and retries don't provide status feedback. Users see delays without understanding why.
incorrectExplanations.d: Pre-computing is impossible for dynamic operations like inventory checks and payment processing that depend on real-time state.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Long-running operations with status feedback = return of control

questions.examTip: Return of control enables handling long-running operations in agents while maintaining conversational flow and user feedback.

questions.scenario: A document processing system receives PDFs that need to be converted to text, analyzed for key information, and stored with extracted metadata. The processing pipeline uses AWS services. The team wants to trigger Bedrock analysis automatically when new PDFs arrive in S3.
questions.question: Which event-driven architecture triggers Bedrock processing when new documents arrive?

options.text: Configure S3 Event Notifications to invoke a Lambda function that calls Bedrock for analysis
Use Amazon Textract to extract text and automatically forward to Bedrock
Set up a scheduled Lambda function that polls S3 for new files every minute
Configure CloudWatch Events to monitor S3 bucket metrics and trigger processing

questions.explanation: S3 Event Notifications provide immediate, event-driven triggering when objects are created. The Lambda function can orchestrate the full pipeline: text extraction, Bedrock analysis, and metadata storage. This is the standard pattern for S3-triggered processing.

incorrectExplanations.b: Textract doesn't automatically forward to Bedrock. It extracts text but requires additional orchestration for the Bedrock analysis step.
incorrectExplanations.c: Polling adds unnecessary latency (up to 1 minute) and consumes resources even when no new files exist. Event-driven is more efficient.
incorrectExplanations.d: CloudWatch Events/EventBridge can receive S3 events but adds an extra hop. Direct S3 Event Notifications to Lambda is simpler for this use case.

parseStrategy.keyPhrase: trigger automatically when new PDFs arrive
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: S3 arrival triggers processing = S3 Event Notifications to Lambda

questions.examTip: S3 Event Notifications to Lambda is the standard pattern for object-arrival-triggered processing.

questions.scenario: A machine learning team has fine-tuned an open-source LLM for their specific use case and needs to deploy it for production inference. They require control over the inference infrastructure, including the ability to optimize batch sizes and use custom inference code. The model is 13B parameters.
questions.question: Which deployment option provides the MOST control over inference infrastructure for custom models?

options.text: Import the model to Amazon Bedrock using Custom Model Import
Deploy on Amazon SageMaker with a custom inference container and endpoint configuration
Host the model on Amazon EC2 instances with manual load balancing
Use Amazon Bedrock fine-tuning to adapt a base model to the use case

questions.explanation: SageMaker provides full control over inference infrastructure while managing the underlying instances. Custom inference containers allow custom code, and endpoint configuration controls batch sizes, instance types, and scaling. This balances control with managed infrastructure.

incorrectExplanations.a: Bedrock Custom Model Import hosts models but doesn't provide the same level of infrastructure control as SageMaker. You can't customize inference code or batch optimization.
incorrectExplanations.c: EC2 with manual load balancing provides maximum control but loses the managed inference benefits. More operational overhead without additional capability.
incorrectExplanations.d: Bedrock fine-tuning starts with Bedrock base models, not custom open-source models. It doesn't address the bring-your-own-model requirement.

eliminationHints: Bedrock fine-tuning = wrong starting point (not BYOM)

questions.examTip: SageMaker is the answer when you need infrastructure control for custom models. Bedrock is for using provided foundation models.

questions.scenario: A financial services company is building an AI agent that provides investment recommendations. The agent must access market data APIs, portfolio information, and risk analysis tools. Due to regulatory requirements, every recommendation must be traceable to its source data and reasoning, and human approval is required before any trade execution.
questions.question: Which Bedrock Agent configuration addresses both the auditability and human approval requirements?

options.text: Enable agent tracing for full reasoning visibility and configure human-in-the-loop action for trade execution
Log all agent responses to CloudWatch and require users to manually initiate trades through a separate system
Use Step Functions to orchestrate the workflow with manual approval states
Configure guardrails to require confirmation before any financial action

questions.explanation: Agent tracing captures the full reasoning path—which tools were called, what data was retrieved, how the agent reached its conclusion. Human-in-the-loop action configuration requires explicit human approval before the trade execution action proceeds. This addresses both requirements within the agent architecture.

incorrectExplanations.b: Logging responses doesn't capture the reasoning path. Separate manual trading loses the integration benefits of the agent. This is a workaround, not a solution.
incorrectExplanations.c: Step Functions could work but requires rebuilding the agent workflow outside of Bedrock Agents. The question asks about Bedrock Agent configuration.
incorrectExplanations.d: Guardrails are for content safety, not workflow approval. They filter content, not orchestrate human approvals.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Agent auditability = tracing. Human approval = human-in-the-loop action

questions.examTip: Agent tracing provides auditability of reasoning. Human-in-the-loop enables approval gates for sensitive actions.
